data.title: Euro zone long-dated yields resume their grind higher
data.description:
Euro zone bond yields resumed their climb on Monday, reversing a U.S. Federal Reserve-driven fall from late last week as traders reassessed that move and any Fed impact on Europe, and processed data showing an uptick in German business morale.

Germany's 10-year bond yield, the benchmark for the euro zone, was up 5 basis points at 2.77%, (DE10YT=RR) heading back towards last week's near five-month top of 2.787%.

Germany's 30-year yield rose nearly 6 bps to 3.36% and was just a whisker off last week's 14-year high. (DE30YT=RR)

"The mild upward trajectory since June in long-end European government bond yields remains intact," said Kenneth Broux head of corporate research FX and rates at Societe Generale.

Longer-dated yields have been rising around the world as investors grow nervous over governments' high levels of debt, or in Germany's case, a large expansion in its borrowing.

That trend saw a fairly rare interruption last Friday, as Fed chair Jerome Powell, in his final address as Fed chair at the Jackson Hole economic symposium, hinted at a September interest rate cut.

While he stopped short of clearly committing to such a move, his comments were sufficient to spark a rally in stocks and U.S. Treasuries that also spilled over into Europe, sending German 10-year yields down around 4 bps.

However, yields' rising trend resumed on Monday, partly because the European Central Bank, which has cut rates by much more than the Fed so far this cycle, is now in a different place.

Remarks by ECB policymakers at Jackson Hole were "consistent with an extended pause," Jim Reid, global head of macro research at Deutsche Bank, said in a note.

ECB President Christine Lagarde avoided discussing the policy outlook but highlighted the resilience of the euro area labour market.

U.S. Treasury yields also rose around 2 basis points across the curve as traders calibrated their positioning.

Back in Europe, data on Monday that showed German business morale unexpectedly improved in August to its best level in 15 months further underpinned the rise in yields and offered a similar message to last week's upbeat business activity data.

That helps reduce the pressure on the ECB to cut rates again this cycle. Markets see a further cut this year as unlikely, though they do see a reasonable chance of a move by the early part of next year. (0#EURIRPR)

Italy's 10-year yield moved largely in line with Germany's, up nearly 6 bps to 3.61%.

Germany's two-year yield was up 3 bps at 1.98%, slightly steepening the country's yield curve. (DE2YT=RR)

Canada's Big Banks Expected to See Revenue Boost From Solid Capital Markets — Analysis
data.description:
(11:17 GMT) *Big Canadian Banks Expected to Show Boost to Revenue From Solid Quarter for Credit Markets
(11:17 GMT) *Analysts Expect Most Big Canadian Banks to Continue Buying Back Shares
(11:17 GMT) Canada's Big Banks Expected to See Revenue Boost From Solid Capital Markets — Analysis

By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada's biggest banks are likely set to post revenue growth following a solid quarter for credit markets and a recovery in dealmaking.

Third-quarter earnings for Canada's Big Six are due to be released over three days this week, beginning with numbers Tuesday from Bank of Montreal and Bank of Nova Scotia.

Analysts broadly expect trends from earlier in the year to have continued in the latest three months, with signs of stability seen in credit quality, modest loan growth and strength in capital markets and wealth businesses.

"Industry data and U.S. bank results imply that it should be another good quarter for trading revenue and that investment banking activity has started to improve," Paul Holden, an analyst at CIBC Capital Markets, said.

Based on forecasts compiled by FactSet, the banks are expected to post an average 5.1% rise in core earnings per share and 7.6% revenue growth for the quarter versus a year earlier.

Analysis by Morningstar DBRS points to another quarter in which capital markets offered a revenue boost for the banks, after these business segments climbed 19% on year in the first half of the fiscal year thanks to strength in both trading and underwriting and advisory operations.

Morningstar notes Canadian equity-trading volumes remained high in the third quarter, up 21% on a year earlier. Derivative trading volumes also rose and were up 16% on year thanks to political and economic instability and falling interest rates.

Healthy deal activity at the same time continues to drive investment banking, despite what the firm said were fears of a drop in dealmaking amid heightened tariff uncertainty, with global volumes in M&A and equity capital markets improving through early August.

The quarterly earnings will land with a mixed backdrop for Canada's lenders.

Economic activity has slowed sharply after a strong start to 2025, but the country has so far avoided the recession that many economists worried would come quickly thanks to the abrupt shift in U.S. trade policy and President Trump's aggressive tariffs. The unemployment rate has risen steadily, while job losses have largely been focused on industries tied closely with trade such as manufacturing. And while factory sales have fallen and the housing market has remained sluggish, household consumption has continued to be resilient, with retail sales growing modestly in the three months through June.

Canada also has taken a step toward easing trade tensions with the U.S. Prime Minister Mark Carney on Friday moved to smooth relations with President Trump, announcing plans to remove retaliatory tariffs of 25% on about half of the U.S. goods that had been targeted since March. Already, recent polls were showing consumer and business confidence was slowly recovering from record lows in the spring.

Darko Mihelic, analyst at RBC Capital Markets, said that despite softness in gross domestic product, unemployment and housing market trends, these aren't likely to be problem spots in third-quarter results and credit quality should be stable. For some banks, consumer-delinquency rates may even show some improvement from the prior quarter.

Mihelic's modeling sees about an 11% rise in credit-loss provisions from last year across the banks, excluding parent Royal Bank of Canada, but an average drop of almost 14% from the prior quarter.

Most of the big banks have been buying back stock given that they maintain solid capital buffers and liquidity positions, with the exception of National Bank, which has been integrating its recent acquisition of Western Bank. Analysts expect the purchases to continue, and they will be looking for bank executives to signal that.

"In the near term, we expect to see share repurchases continue at a similar pace given the dearth of loan growth," CIBC's Holden said, pointing to both strong capital positions and regulatory capital requirements that aren't expected to rise and should give lenders the confidence to trim capital ratios that sit comfortably above the minimum needed.
